author: @MichaelSkinner-e9j
text:
I agree with you you don’t need a whole lot of money, but what you do need is some money.

You can’t make graphene if you’re incredibly poor. You could take some tape and strip it off that way, but if you want to make it industrially on a large scale, you do need a lot of money, especially flush graphene on a large scale.

Compared to when I worked at Braman, I am incredibly poor

author: @allenhammer7923
text: Please turn up your audio and stop whispering. Your one of the few I have to have headphones on to understand you. You sometimes change descriptions with other words seeming to mean the same thing. I really appreciate what you are trying to accomplish. I wonder if you can use AC power and ultrasonic bath with whey to break apart layers and maybe if need be it could be done in a blender. Ultrasonic blender with alternating current with the Whey keeping layers apart they spay it onto a copper plate with a DC current and see what forms? Time of spray and equal density of spay what you might be able to acquire? Just a thought. I did see one video of what they said was a large sheet of single layer graphite which acted like cellophane but much stronger. How they made it I have no idea.

author: @jasonmorello1374
text: The doing it at home thing is exactly my angle as well. I was looking into things to do it with carbon nanotubes (cnt's), but this will save significantly. I am basically looking at ways to make graphene to shape and order, perhaps intergrating 3d printer forms and some method for hardening. I am looking at geometric manipulation to add rigidity and other ways to focus on flexibility. I plan to integrate this with ideas like nylon spinners to make super cables, conducters and more. I am really thinking this kind of idea can bring invention back to a one man home lab.

author: @candyarellano1251
text: I have a question. I am doing a research paper on desalination. could graphene be used to create an ion depletion zone? I've heard about it being used as a membrane but i wanted to see if it can be used that way.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it can but oddly enough it is more efficient if used in conjunction with a layer of graphene oxide - i work on desalination too as it happens

replies.author: @candyarellano1251
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith have you seen the chip that was created at UT that was able to desalinate nanoliter of sea water without a membrane using 3 volts? I was wondering if the chip was made of graphene instead you could have the chip itself provide the charge instead of having to use a batter?

replies.author: @candyarellano1251
replies.text: Candy Arellano it's called electrochemically medicated seawater desalination. Also i am obviously new chemistry so how would a layer of graphene oxide improve the efficiency?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Candy Arellano
ok interesting musings but ECMS has a long way to go. Ion depletion is useful in loads of things like solar cells and transistors a quick review of papers on solar cells will show you the importance of GO in ion depletion. If this could be turned to ECMS it would indeed be interesting.

replies.author: @candyarellano1251
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Thank you so much for answering!!

author: @TheIdeanator
text:
Excellent explanation on how it all works. I have yet more questions, when you lay the graphene or GO solutions on a "flat" surface and dry it, how to the graphene flakes orient themselves? Do they lay with the c axis parallel or perpendicular to it? Would I be able to verify experimental results with home accessible or inexpensive testing gear?
Also, when they grow graphene sheets, do you know if they are using monocrystaline copper?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The iodide ion is large so the negative charge is spread over a large space. The weaker ionic interaction in HI makes dissociation of the proton from the anion easier so HI is the strongest of the hydrohalides. But,if you are used to HCl, HBr and HF you're going to be fine - lots of care and preferably in a fume cupboard or outside with gloves and glasses. Unfortunately HI is a class 1 listed chemical in the US so it's sale and distribution is restricted - you might have trouble getting it.

author: @jaminbarefoot
text: Just get an Ella Fitzgerald record and crank it up!

author: @jaminbarefoot
text: You could use a home stereo with the frequencies recorded in stereo and look for the right frequencies using home recording equipment and a frequency generator.

author: @jaminbarefoot
text: Yeah so instead of using graphite powder get a chunk and suspend it in the Hummer solution. Instead of using such high concentration dilute it a little or a lot depending, You still need to make the wedges, but heres the thing, a sound source points at the side and maybe you'd have one at the bottom. You could attenuate the frequency on the side source really high and the one below lower. The side speaker would shove the reactant in gently, bottom would jostle.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: incidentally - there is no cvd home based method out there - it's just too complicated for the home lab. I think you could set one up - a home cvd kit and the project would be very interesting to lots of people - but there isn't one at the moment - well as far as I am aware.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: You can use lots of things - the favored lab method is hydrazine. But that is dangerous and controlled - you probably won't be able to buy it. Eric uses a flash bulb from a camera and a laserscribe writer. You can also use aluminium. hope this helps.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Eric Goeken - he's on my likes list - first three vids I think. It's not an easier method though - it's the second part where he puts the GO onto a cd and laserscribes it

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I was thinking about this - and to be honest i think nucleation would still be a problem as it is a complex system - but hey - this is just my guess.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: it is if you have a laser. Remember this channel is all about the average home inventor. More people have microwaves and know how to use them than have lasers. The challenge is not so much on how to produce graphene - that's pretty much a solved issue to be honest - but how to produce it at home with the minimum equipment and materials. Now that's a different story and pretty challenging.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: yeah - it's more expensive than gold! - my wife keeps telling me I should be selling this stuff to fund my research and I have to say - i've been thinking about it.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: turning into liquid in solid, milk - solid in liquid - and so. colloidal dispersions are pretty stable.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Nothing at all - and when you dry it that's pretty much what happens which is why GO paper is so strong. I kept calling it a solution - i realised when i listened back. It's really a suspension. The particles are so thin that the forces in the water keep it in suspension and that is what holds it apart and makes it a gel. It's very similar to a colloid - a colloid is where one phase is dispersed through another. For example bread - gas in solid, fog - liquid in gas, jelly - solid in liquid

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I didn't make a video of the GO cleaning process - I figured it was too boring! I have been asked to redo the whole process again and video it so I might well do that and include the washing steps. I'll put together some tang lau stuff togethr for you and let you know - I listened to your videos incidentally - good music - cheers.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I like your sound idea. Sonochemistry is a very valid route for adding energy to a reaction. What do you specifically have in mind? Mostly ultrasound is used. Though I have been thinking about high energy cavitation acoustically generated. I suspect though it would be very uncontrolled - but you don't know until you try - right? What you seem to be thinking is some kind of acoustic levitation scheme? I'mnot sure but I would certainly be interested in knowing more.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: bad - just in different ways.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Unfortunately - like all of these things there is a limit. Ultrasound as a pure method of graphene production just does too much damage. it's not just that the bits are so small they are also distorted. It doesn't mean that the product of ultrasound generated graphene is useless - far from it - but when looking at graphene production strategies what you have to get clear first is why you want the graphene - what purpose do you want it to serve - the variety of ways of making it are each good and

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: good work. I subscribed to you so I can keep up with your progress.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Thanks mate. To be honest the reaction is really simple and not a lot else can happen - if it happens. It's a fairly standard reaction called the hummers method and is used labs all over the world to make graphene oxide. You will know it has been successful after you add the hydrogen peroxide - it should go bright yellow - the more yellow it is the more successful you have been. Then it is just a matter of washing it and reducing it back to graphene from the graphene oxide

author: @MYNICEEV
text:
First off Robert. I prefer to be taught by someone who can teach at my level (I know nothing) and teach me as my level of knowledge increases. I have no time for people who are condescending of tutors who are able to teach from the bottom all the way to the top. Those people if they are tutors need to re-learn their job. And those who aren't tutors. Well they can go back to basics too.
Now for this graphene. If I had a go at making some. How could I detect it? To know that I had succeeded.
